footer.tagline: Shariah-compliant vehicle financing contracts for Muslim buyers and sellers in the United States.
footer.disclaimer: AqadChain provides tools for structuring vehicle sales as Murabaha or Musawama agreements. This platform does not provide legal or religious advice. Users are responsible for ensuring compliance with applicable laws and consulting qualified Shariah advisors. Contracts are governed by California state law.

steps.title: Create your contract

contractTypes.label: Contract types
contractTypes.title: Three structures.
contractTypes.subtitle: Choose the structure that fits your deal — sale or lease, cost-disclosed or negotiated.

murabaha.title: Murabaha
murabaha.subtitle: Cost-Plus Financing
murabaha.description: The seller discloses their purchase cost and agrees on a fixed profit margin. The most transparent and widely used Islamic finance structure.

musawama.title: Musawama
musawama.subtitle: Negotiated Price Sale
musawama.description: Both parties negotiate a final price — no cost disclosure required. Equally Shariah-compliant. More privacy for the seller.

ijarah.title: Ijarah
ijarah.subtitle: Islamic Lease
ijarah.description: The lessor owns the vehicle and leases it to the lessee. Rental payments cover usufruct only. Optionally ends with ownership transfer (IMIT).

lateFee.title: Late fees without Riba
lateFee.body1: If a payment is more than 30 days late, a fixed daily fee applies — but <0>the seller does not keep it</0>. It goes to a charity both parties agree on at signing.
lateFee.body2: This creates real accountability without the seller profiting from tardiness, which would constitute Riba. Common choices include Islamic Relief USA, Zakat Foundation, or a local masjid.

pricingTeaser.subtitle: Contracts live in-platform. Access is always controlled. Cancel anytime.

faq.question: Is there a free trial?
faq.answer: You can create an account and start a contract for free. A subscription is required to send contracts for signature.

faq.question: Can I cancel anytime?
faq.answer: Yes. Cancel your subscription anytime from your account settings. You will retain access until the end of the billing period.

faq.question: Are there per-contract fees?
faq.answer: No — your subscription covers unlimited access for your plan tier. No surprise fees per document.

faq.question: What payment methods do you accept?
faq.answer: We accept all major credit and debit cards via Stripe. ACH bank transfers are available on request for Business plans.

howItWorks.heroSubtitle: A step-by-step guide to creating your Halal vehicle financing contract.

steps.title: Create an account
steps.body: Sign up with Google or your email. Account creation takes under a minute. After signup, a short onboarding flow explains how the platform works.

steps.title: Choose your contract type
steps.body: Select Murabaha (cost-plus, cost disclosed to buyer) or Musawama (negotiated price, no cost disclosure required). Both are valid Shariah-compliant structures.

steps.title: Enter vehicle details
steps.body: Type in the VIN and we'll auto-fill the year, make, model, and trim using the free NHTSA public database. We also check for any active safety recalls and show you a warning badge.

steps.title: Add buyer and seller information
steps.body: Enter names, emails, phone numbers, and addresses for both parties. If you're the seller, your account details can be auto-filled.

steps.title: Set deal terms
steps.body: Enter the car price, down payment, markup percentage (capped at 25%), financing term, and payment frequency. A live calculator shows the monthly payment and total payable amount as you type.

steps.title: Review the contract
steps.body: Preview the full contract document before sending — including the payment schedule, all clauses, and your signature blocks. Make any edits needed.

steps.title: Send for e-signatures
steps.body: With one click, both parties receive a DocuSign link by email. They can sign from any device — no printing, scanning, or in-person meeting required.

steps.title: Signed and done
steps.body: Once both parties sign, the completed PDF is securely stored and accessible from your dashboard. You'll receive a confirmation email with a link to download.

howItWorks.murabahaBody1: In a Murabaha transaction, the seller discloses their actual purchase price and the buyer agrees to a fixed markup above that price. The final selling price is the cost plus the agreed markup.
howItWorks.murabahaExample: <0>Example:</0> Seller purchased a car for $20,000. They agree on a 10% markup ($2,000). The buyer pays $22,000 total — typically in monthly installments over 24–60 months.
howItWorks.musawamaBody1: Musawama is a general sale where both parties negotiate and agree on a price without the seller being required to disclose their cost. It's the same end result, but without mandatory cost transparency.
howItWorks.musawamaExample: <0>Example:</0> A buyer and seller agree the car is worth $22,000. No cost disclosure is required. Buyer pays in installments. Equally valid under Islamic finance principles.
howItWorks.lateFeeBody1: Traditional interest on late payments is Riba (forbidden in Islam). AqadChain handles this through a <0>charitable penalty</0>: if a buyer is more than 30 days late on a payment, a fixed daily fee applies.
howItWorks.lateFeeBody2: Critically, <0>the seller does not keep this fee as income</0>. It is donated to a charity that both parties agree on at the time of signing. This creates accountability (late fees are real consequences) without the seller profiting from tardiness.
howItWorks.lateFeeBody3: The charity is chosen during contract creation and written into the contract. Common choices include local mosques, Islamic Relief, Zakat Foundation, or any 501(c)(3) of the parties' choice.
howItWorks.disclaimer: AqadChain provides software tools only and does not issue fatwas or Shariah certifications. The contract structures are inspired by AAOIFI standards but have not been certified by a Shariah board. Users are encouraged to consult a qualified Shariah advisor before entering into any transaction. Contracts are legally governed by California state law.
howItWorks.ctaSubtitle: Create your first Halal vehicle financing contract in minutes.

about.title: About AqadChain
about.subtitle: Making Halal vehicle financing accessible to every Muslim in America.
about.mission1: Muslim Americans who want to finance vehicle purchases without Riba (interest) have historically had few options. Traditional auto loans involve interest — forbidden under Islamic law. Community-based arrangements exist but often lack legal structure, leaving both buyer and seller exposed.
about.mission2: AqadChain was built to solve this. We provide the tools to create legally binding, Shariah-inspired vehicle financing contracts between individuals — with professional e-signatures, secure storage, and a guided experience that takes minutes, not days.
about.approach1: We take a practical, transparent approach. Our contracts are modeled on the Murabaha and Musawama structures described in AAOIFI Shari'ah Standards — the most widely referenced Islamic finance standards globally.
about.approach2: We are upfront that AqadChain is a software platform, not a Shariah certification body. We do not issue fatwas. We provide a legally enforceable contract framework inspired by Islamic finance principles, and we encourage users to consult a qualified Shariah advisor before entering any transaction.
about.advisorBody: Shariah advisor partnerships are being established. This section will be updated once our advisory board is finalized.
about.bayAreaBody: We launched AqadChain in the San Francisco Bay Area — home to one of the largest and most diverse Muslim communities in the United States. Our contracts are structured under California law and designed for private-party vehicle sales within the US.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about AqadChain and Halal vehicle financing.

items.question: What makes a car financing contract Halal?
items.answer: A Halal (permissible) financing contract avoids Riba (interest). Instead of a loan with interest, the seller and buyer agree on a sale price with a fixed markup. The buyer pays in installments. The profit comes from a genuine sale, not from lending money.

items.question: What is Murabaha?
items.answer: Murabaha is a cost-plus sale structure where the seller discloses their actual purchase cost and agrees on a fixed profit margin with the buyer. The buyer pays the total (cost + markup) in deferred installments. No interest is charged.

items.question: What is Musawama?
items.answer: Musawama is a negotiated-price sale where both parties agree on a final price without the seller disclosing their cost. It is equally Shariah-compliant — cost disclosure is optional under Musawama.

items.question: Is a markup the same as interest?
items.answer: No. In Islamic finance, the key distinction is that profit comes from a genuine commercial transaction (buying and selling a tangible asset) rather than from lending money over time. The markup is agreed upon upfront and fixed — it does not grow if the buyer is late (unlike interest). Late fees are donated to charity, not kept as income.

items.question: Do I need a lawyer to use AqadChain?
items.answer: No. AqadChain generates a complete, legally structured contract automatically. You do not need to hire a lawyer to create or sign a contract on our platform. However, for large transactions, legal counsel is always a prudent choice.

items.question: How does e-signing work?
items.answer: After you create a contract, both parties receive an email from DocuSign — the industry-leading e-signature provider. They click the link, review the contract, and sign electronically. Signatures are legally binding under the ESIGN Act.

items.question: Can both parties sign from their phones?
items.answer: Yes. DocuSign works on any device — phone, tablet, or desktop. No app download is required.

items.question: What happens after both parties sign?
items.answer: The signed PDF is securely stored and emailed to both parties. It is also accessible from your AqadChain dashboard for download at any time.

items.question: Is this legally enforceable in California?
items.answer: Yes. AqadChain contracts are structured as standard deferred-payment sale agreements governed by California law. They include all legally required clauses for secured vehicle sales and are executed via compliant e-signatures.

items.question: Does AqadChain provide Shariah certification?
items.answer: No. AqadChain is a software platform that provides contract templates inspired by AAOIFI Islamic finance standards. We do not issue fatwas or Shariah certifications. Users should consult a qualified Shariah advisor for religious guidance.

items.question: What is the maximum markup percentage?
items.answer: AqadChain caps markup at 25% to prevent predatory terms. This is a platform-level safeguard — parties may agree on any lower markup.

items.question: How does the late fee work without charging interest?
items.answer: If a payment is more than 30 days late, a fixed daily fee applies — but the seller does not keep this fee. It is donated to a charity both parties agreed on at signing. This creates accountability without the seller profiting from tardiness (which would resemble Riba).
